title_en: Taharah in Al-Hidayah: Hanafi Purification with Evidential Basis
title_ar: الطهارة في الهداية: الطهارة الحنفية مع أدلتها
content_en:
Al-Hidayah's taharah sections provide the evidential basis for the Hanafi positions stated tersely in Bidayat al-Mubtadi. Al-Marghinani explains each ruling with its Quranic or hadith foundation and often engages with the arguments of other schools, making the taharah chapter of Al-Hidayah both a legal reference and an introduction to comparative Islamic purification law.

On the purifying capacity of water, Al-Hidayah explains the Hanafi position with reference to the Quranic command to use 'pure water' (25:48) and the prophetic statement 'Water is pure and nothing makes it impure.' The latter hadith, while its chain of transmission was subject to some scholarly discussion, is understood by the Hanafi school as establishing the general principle that water's purifying capacity is robust and is not easily lost — only actual contamination that changes the water's character overcomes this robustness.

The Hanafi school's position on musta'mal (used) water is explained in Al-Hidayah with reference to the reasoning that the act of purification 'transfers' the hadath (ritual impurity) to the water, rendering it 'used' in a specific sense. Al-Marghinani engages with the Maliki counter-argument that used water retains its purifying capacity, presenting the Hanafi position's basis while acknowledging the scholarly debate.

For wudu', Al-Hidayah provides the evidential basis for the four Hanafi obligatory elements with reference to the Quranic verse on ablution (5:6). Al-Marghinani explains why the Hanafi school requires wiping of at least one quarter of the head — derived from the principle that the 'ba'' in 'wa-msahu bi-ru'usikum' indicates that partial wiping satisfies the Quranic obligation. He engages with the Maliki and Hanbali schools' requirement of full head-wiping and explains the different interpretive approaches to the same Quranic text.

The sections on wiping over leather socks (khuffayn) in Al-Hidayah present the evidential basis — the widely-transmitted prophetic hadith giving a specific time limit for wiping — and address the conditions for the permission's application. Al-Marghinani notes the extraordinary strength of the hadith evidence for sock-wiping (transmitted by over seventy Companions according to some accounts) and uses this as an example of how an apparently lax practice is in fact firmly grounded in the prophetic Sunnah.

Al-Hidayah's discussion of the obligatory elements of ghusl with their evidential basis — including the distinctive Hanafi requirement of rinsing the mouth and nostrils — provides students with the school's reasoning for these requirements. Al-Marghinani draws on the prophetic descriptions of ghusl in which rinsing the mouth and nostrils are consistently mentioned and argues that the prophetic practice, combined with the principle that the inside of natural body openings is part of the 'body' to be washed, grounds the Hanafi obligation.

The taharah sections of Al-Hidayah exemplify the work's distinctive contribution: by providing the evidential basis for Hanafi positions, it transforms purification law from a received tradition into a reasoned system that students can understand, evaluate, and apply.
